seoTitle: HGSEMI Application Solutions | Power Management System Design
seoDescription: Complete HGSEMI application solutions including battery-powered systems, industrial power supplies, and LED lighting designs from authorized distributor.

faqs.question: What application solutions does HGSEMI provide?
faqs.answer: HGSEMI provides comprehensive application solutions for power management: 1) Battery-powered systems - complete power management for portable devices including charging, regulation, and protection, 2) Industrial power supplies - robust power solutions for industrial automation and control systems, 3) LED lighting - efficient drivers for general lighting, automotive, and display applications, 4) Automotive electronics - AEC-Q100 qualified devices for automotive power management. Each solution includes reference designs, BOM recommendations, and technical documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select solution based on your application requirements. Contact FAE for customized solution recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I design a complete battery-powered system?
faqs.answer: Battery-powered system design involves: 1) Battery selection - choose chemistry (Li-ion, LiFePO4, NiMH) based on energy density and cycle life requirements, 2) Charger IC - select charging IC matching battery chemistry with appropriate charge current, 3) Voltage regulation - use LDO or switching regulator to provide stable system voltage, 4) Protection - implement over-charge, over-discharge, and over-current protection, 5) Power path - consider power path management for simultaneous charge and system operation, 6) Fuel gauge - add battery monitoring for accurate capacity indication. HGSEMI provides all necessary ICs for complete battery management systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match components to battery chemistry and system requirements. Always include protection ICs for safety.

faqs.question: What are the key considerations for industrial power supply design?
faqs.answer: Industrial power supply design considerations: 1) Input voltage range - industrial systems often have wide input ranges (9-36V or 18-75V), 2) Isolation - many industrial applications require galvanic isolation for safety, 3) Protection - over-voltage, over-current, and reverse polarity protection essential, 4) Temperature range - industrial grade components rated for -40°C to +85°C, 5) EMI compliance - meet industrial EMC standards, 6) Reliability - use high-quality components for long service life, 7) Efficiency - minimize heat generation in enclosed enclosures. HGSEMI offers industrial-grade regulators and protection devices for these demanding applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use industrial grade components. Plan for wide input ranges and temperature extremes. Include comprehensive protection.

faqs.question: What are the key parameters for LED driver selection?
faqs.answer: LED driver selection parameters: 1) LED current - match driver current rating to LED requirements, 2) Input voltage - ensure driver supports your input voltage range, 3) Output voltage - sum of LED forward voltages in series string, 4) Topology - constant current for discrete LEDs, constant voltage for strips, 5) Dimming - PWM or analog dimming support if needed, 6) Protection - over-temperature, open/short LED protection, 7) Efficiency - higher efficiency reduces heat generation. Calculate total LED power and ensure driver has adequate margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match driver specifications to LED requirements. Consider dimming and protection features. Plan for thermal management.

faqs.question: How do I design a complete battery management system?
faqs.answer: Battery management system design: 1) Charger IC - select based on battery chemistry (Li-ion, LiFePO4, NiMH) and charge current, 2) Protection IC - provides over-charge, over-discharge, over-current protection, 3) Fuel Gauge - monitors battery capacity and state of charge, 4) Power Path - allows simultaneous charging and system operation, 5) Cell balancing - for multi-cell packs to ensure equal cell voltage. Consider battery capacity, charge time requirements, and safety standards. Always include protection ICs for safety.
faqs.decisionGuide: Include charger, protection, and fuel gauge for complete BMS. Select components based on battery chemistry and capacity.

faqs.question: What thermal considerations are important for power management ICs?
faqs.answer: Thermal design considerations: 1) Power dissipation - calculate P = (Vin - Vout) × Iout for LDOs, use efficiency for switching regulators, 2) Junction temperature - Tj = Ta + (P × θja), keep below maximum rating (usually 125°C or 150°C), 3) Heat sinking - copper area on PCB acts as heatsink, thermal vias help spread heat, 4) Airflow - forced airflow significantly improves cooling, 5) Layout - place heat-generating components away from sensitive circuits. Use thermal simulation for high-power designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation and junction temperature. Use adequate copper area and thermal vias. Consider forced airflow for high power.

solutions.title: Battery-Powered Portable System
solutions.subtitle: Complete power management solution for portable electronics with Li-ion battery
solutions.description: Comprehensive battery-powered system solution featuring Li-ion battery charging, voltage regulation, and protection for portable electronics applications.
solutions.longDescription: This complete battery-powered system solution provides everything needed for portable electronics applications. The design centers around a single-cell Li-ion battery with integrated charging, voltage regulation, and system protection. The HG6210 linear charger provides a simple, low-cost charging solution with programmable charge current up to 1A. System power is regulated by the HG7533 ultra-low quiescent current LDO, which consumes only 45μA, maximizing battery life. The HG8101 battery protection IC provides essential safety features including over-charge, over-discharge, and over-current protection. This solution is ideal for IoT sensors, handheld devices, portable medical equipment, and wireless communication devices. The entire power management subsystem consumes less than 100μA in standby mode, enabling years of operation from a single battery charge.
benefits: Ultra-low standby current extends battery life
Complete protection for safe battery operation
Simple design with minimal external components
features: Complete battery management in minimal BOM
Ultra-low power consumption extends battery life
coreAdvantages: Ultra-low quiescent current of 45μA maximizes battery life
Integrated protection prevents battery damage and ensures safety
Minimal BOM with only 3 ICs reduces cost and complexity
Compact footprint fits space-constrained portable designs
Programmable charge current allows optimization for different batteries

faeInsights.author: Michael Zhang
faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Power Management
faeInsights.content: This battery-powered system solution is one of our most popular reference designs. I've helped dozens of customers implement this exact architecture for IoT and portable applications. The key to success is the HG7533's ultra-low quiescent current - at only 45μA, it allows sensors to operate for years on a single battery charge. The HG6210 charger is simple and reliable, though for faster charging or USB-C applications, you might consider the HG6220 switching charger. One important tip: always include the HG8101 protection IC. While some designers try to save cost by omitting it, the protection IC is essential for safety and battery longevity. I've seen batteries damaged from over-discharge when protection was omitted. For the PCB layout, keep the battery traces short and wide, and place the protection IC as close to the battery connector as possible. This design is ideal for applications requiring >6 months battery life on a single charge.
keyTakeaways: The HG7533's 45μA quiescent current enables multi-year battery life
Always include HG8101 protection IC for safety and battery longevity
Place protection IC close to battery connector with short wide traces
Consider HG6220 for applications requiring fast charging

decisionFramework.title: Battery-Powered System Selection Decision Framework
steps: Define battery life requirement: >6 months = use HG7533 architecture, <6 months = consider higher current options
Select battery chemistry: Li-ion for energy density, LiFePO4 for cycle life and safety
Determine charge current: 100mA for small batteries, 500mA-1A for larger capacity batteries
Verify protection: Always include HG8101 for safety compliance
Plan PCB layout: Keep battery traces short and wide, protection IC near battery connector

customerCases.challenge: Battery-powered sensors needed 2+ year operation without battery replacement
customerCases.solution: Implemented complete battery management system using HG6210 charger, HG7533 regulator, and HG8101 protection
customerCases.result: Achieved 3+ year battery life with 15-minute sampling interval, exceeding customer requirements
customerCases.feedback: The solution exceeded our battery life requirements. The HG7533's low quiescent current was the key factor in achieving multi-year operation.

customerCases.challenge: Fitness tracker needed compact power solution with 7-day battery life
customerCases.solution: Used HG7533 LDO and HG8101 protection in compact 10mm x 10mm footprint
customerCases.result: Achieved compact design with 7+ day battery life in 100mAh battery
customerCases.feedback: The compact solution allowed us to meet our stringent size constraints while maintaining adequate battery life for a fitness tracking application.

faqs.question: What battery chemistries are supported by this solution?
faqs.answer: This solution supports single-cell lithium-based batteries: 1) Li-ion (LiCoO2) - most common, 3.7V nominal, 4.2V charge voltage, 2) Li-Polymer - same chemistry as Li-ion in pouch format, 3) LiFePO4 - with modified charge voltage (3.6V instead of 4.2V). The HG6210 charger is specifically designed for Li-ion/Li-Po with 4.2V charge voltage. For LiFePO4, use HG6210F variant with 3.6V charge voltage. The protection IC and regulator work with all lithium chemistries. Not suitable for NiMH or lead-acid batteries which require different charging profiles.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard HG6210 for Li-ion/Li-Po. Use HG6210F for LiFePO4 batteries.

faqs.question: How do I program the charge current for HG6210?
faqs.answer: HG6210 charge current programming: The charge current is set by a resistor connected to the PROG pin. Formula: Icharge = 1000V / Rprog. Common values: 1) 10kΩ = 100mA charge current, 2) 2kΩ = 500mA charge current, 3) 1kΩ = 1A charge current (maximum). The resistor should be 1% tolerance for accurate current setting. Charge current can be monitored by measuring voltage at PROG pin: Vprog = Icharge × Rprog. The actual charge current also depends on input power availability and battery state. Pre-charge (trickle charge) current is 10% of fast charge current for deeply discharged batteries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select resistor based on desired charge time and input power capacity. Use 1% tolerance resistor.

faqs.question: What is the expected battery life for IoT sensor applications?
faqs.answer: Battery life calculation for IoT sensors: Example with 2000mAh Li-ion battery, 15-minute sampling: 1) Active current - 50mA for 1 second during sampling = 50mAs per sample, 2) Sleep current - HG7533 quiescent (45μA) + MCU sleep (10μA) = 55μA continuous, 3) Daily consumption - Active: (50mAs × 96 samples) / 3600 = 1.33mAh, Sleep: 55μA × 24h = 1.32mAh, Total: ~2.65mAh/day, 4) Battery life - 2000mAh / 2.65mAh/day = 755 days (~2 years). To extend battery life: increase sampling interval, reduce active current, use MCU sleep modes, or add solar harvesting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on active and sleep currents. Extend sampling interval for longer battery life.

faqs.question: How does the protection IC prevent battery damage?
faqs.answer: HG8101 protection features: 1) Over-charge protection - disconnects charger when cell voltage exceeds 4.3V, prevents over-charge damage and thermal runaway, 2) Over-discharge protection - disconnects load when cell voltage drops below 2.4V, prevents deep discharge damage, 3) Over-current protection - disconnects load when current exceeds threshold (typically 3A), prevents damage from short circuits, 4) Short-circuit protection - fast response (<10μs) to short circuit conditions. The protection IC uses a MOSFET switch in series with the battery to disconnect when faults are detected. Recovery is automatic when fault condition is removed. These protections are essential for lithium battery safety and longevity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always include protection IC for lithium batteries. Never operate Li-ion without protection.

faqs.question: Can this solution be modified for USB-C charging?
faqs.answer: USB-C charging modifications: The basic HG6210 charger works with USB-C but without Power Delivery (PD) negotiation: 1) Simple USB-C - connect HG6210 input to USB-C VBUS (5V), works as standard USB charging at 500mA or 1A, 2) USB-C PD - for higher voltage/current charging, add USB-C PD controller (like HUSB238) to negotiate 9V or 12V, then use step-down converter, 3) CC pins - add 5.1kΩ pull-down resistors on CC1 and CC2 for USB-C sink detection. For most IoT applications, simple 5V USB-C charging is sufficient. The HG6220 is a switching charger that supports higher input voltages for faster charging applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use simple USB-C connection for 5V charging. Add PD controller for high-voltage fast charging.

seo.keywords: battery-powered system, Li-ion charger, portable power, HGSEMI solution

solutions.title: Industrial Power Supply System
solutions.subtitle: Robust 24V industrial power supply with wide input range and protection
solutions.description: Industrial-grade power supply solution supporting 9-36V input with multiple regulated outputs, comprehensive protection, and EMI compliance.
solutions.longDescription: This industrial power supply solution provides robust, reliable power conversion for industrial automation and control applications. The design accepts a wide 9-36V DC input, typical of 24V industrial systems with tolerance for voltage variations. The HG34063 multi-topology DC-DC converter generates an intermediate 5V rail with high efficiency. This is followed by HG1117 LDO regulators producing clean 3.3V and 1.8V rails for microcontrollers and communication interfaces. The HG809 voltage supervisor provides power-on reset and brown-out detection. Comprehensive protection includes reverse polarity protection, input over-voltage protection, and output over-current protection. The design meets industrial EMC standards with proper filtering and layout. All components are industrial grade (-40°C to +85°C) ensuring reliable operation in harsh environments.
benefits: Wide input range accommodates industrial voltage variations
Multiple regulated outputs for mixed-voltage systems
Industrial grade components for harsh environments
EMI compliant design meets industrial standards
features: Multiple regulated outputs from single input
coreAdvantages: Wide 9-36V input range handles industrial voltage transients
Multiple voltage rails from single input source
High efficiency reduces heat in enclosed enclosures
Industrial temperature rating for harsh environments

faeInsights.author: David Liu
faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Industrial Applications
faeInsights.content: This industrial power supply design has been deployed in hundreds of industrial control systems. The key to reliability is the wide input range - industrial 24V systems can see anything from 18V to 30V, and this design handles it all. The HG34063 is a proven workhorse for industrial buck conversion. I typically see 85-90% efficiency in this configuration. One critical aspect is the input protection - always include the reverse polarity diode and TVS protection. I've seen systems damaged by field wiring errors. The HG809 supervisor is essential for reliable microcontroller operation - it ensures proper reset sequencing during power-up and brown-out conditions. For the PCB layout, keep the switching loop area small to minimize EMI, and use proper ground planes. The output filters are important for meeting EMC standards - don't omit them. This design is ideal for PLC systems, motor control, and process control applications.
keyTakeaways: Wide 9-36V input range handles industrial voltage variations
Always include reverse polarity and TVS protection for field reliability
HG809 supervisor ensures proper reset sequencing
Minimize switching loop area to reduce EMI

decisionFramework.title: Industrial Power Supply Selection Decision Framework
steps: Verify input voltage range: 9-36V covers most 24V industrial systems
Select converter topology: HG34063 for cost-effective buck conversion
Add protection: Include reverse polarity diode and TVS for field reliability
Plan supervision: Add HG809 for proper reset and brown-out protection
Design for EMI: Minimize switching loop, use ground planes and filters

customerCases.challenge: PLC modules needed reliable power from 24V industrial bus with voltage variations
customerCases.solution: Implemented wide-input power supply using HG34063 buck converter with multiple LDO outputs
customerCases.result: Zero field failures over 3 years of operation in harsh industrial environments
customerCases.feedback: The wide input range design has proven extremely reliable in our industrial installations. The protection features have saved multiple modules from field wiring errors.

customerCases.challenge: Control system needed multiple voltage rails from 24V input in compact enclosure
customerCases.solution: Used HG34063 for 5V main rail, then HG1117 LDOs for 3.3V and 1.8V rails
customerCases.result: Compact multi-rail supply with 85% efficiency, operating reliably at 60°C ambient
customerCases.feedback: The multi-rail solution simplified our power architecture and the high efficiency kept thermal management manageable in our compact enclosure.

faqs.question: What input voltage transients can this design withstand?
faqs.answer: Input transient protection: 1) Continuous operation - 9V to 36V DC for normal operation, 2) Surge protection - TVS diode clamps transients up to 60V for short durations (<100ms), 3) Load dump - can withstand 60V for several hundred milliseconds typical of industrial load dump, 4) Reverse polarity - series diode prevents damage from reverse connection, 5) Brown-out - design continues operating down to 9V, supervisor resets system below 8V. For applications with severe transients (like automotive load dump), consider additional protection or the HG34063HV which supports higher input voltages. Always verify protection adequacy for your specific application environment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add TVS diode for transient protection. Verify protection levels match your application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the buck converter inductor value?
faqs.answer: HG34063 buck converter inductor selection: Formula: L = (Vin - Vout) × Vout / (Vin × f × ΔI). Where: Vin = input voltage, Vout = output voltage, f = switching frequency (typically 100kHz for HG34063), ΔI = inductor ripple current (typically 20-30% of Iout). Example for 24V to 5V at 1A: L = (24-5) × 5 / (24 × 100kHz × 0.3) = 132μH. Standard value 150μH is suitable. Inductor saturation current should be at least 1.5× maximum load current. For HG34063 at 1.5A switch current, use inductor rated for 2A minimum. Shielded inductors recommended for reduced EMI.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate using formula or use online calculator. Select standard value with adequate saturation current.

faqs.question: What EMI considerations are important for industrial applications?
faqs.answer: EMI design considerations: 1) Input filter - add π-filter with electrolytic and ceramic capacitors at input, 2) Output filter - ferrite bead and ceramic capacitor on each output, 3) Switching loop - minimize area of high di/dt loops (input cap to switch to inductor), 4) Grounding - use star grounding, separate power and analog grounds, 5) Shielding - use shielded inductors, consider PCB shielding cans, 6) Layout - keep switching traces short, use ground planes. The HG34063 operates at 100kHz which is below common EMI measurement bands but harmonics must be controlled. Typical industrial applications require EN 55011/CISPR 11 Class A or B compliance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize switching loop area. Use input and output filters. Follow good PCB layout practices.

faqs.question: How does the voltage supervisor improve system reliability?
faqs.answer: HG809 voltage supervisor benefits: 1) Power-on reset - holds processor in reset until supply is stable, prevents erratic startup behavior, 2) Brown-out detection - resets processor if supply drops below threshold, prevents flash corruption and undefined operation, 3) Glitch filtering - ignores brief voltage dips to avoid nuisance resets, 4) Manual reset - optional pushbutton reset input. Threshold options: 2.63V, 2.93V, 3.08V, 4.38V, 4.63V. Select threshold slightly below minimum operating voltage of your processor. For 3.3V systems, use 2.93V or 3.08V threshold. The reset pulse width is typically 200ms, ensuring reliable processor initialization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use voltage supervisor for microcontroller systems. Select threshold based on processor requirements.

faqs.question: Can this design operate at higher ambient temperatures?
faqs.answer: High temperature operation: All components are rated for -40°C to +85°C industrial range. For higher temperatures: 1) Derating - output current must be derated above 70°C ambient, 2) Thermal design - increase copper area, add heatsinks, improve airflow, 3) Component selection - some components available in extended temperature range (-40°C to +125°C), 4) Efficiency - higher temperature reduces efficiency, increasing heat generation. At 85°C ambient with 24V input, 5V/1A output: HG34063 junction temperature may approach 125°C limit. Recommend: reduce load current to 0.5A, increase copper area to 10+ square inches, or add small heatsink to HG34063. For continuous high-temperature operation, consider using HG34063 with external MOSFET for better thermal management.
faqs.decisionGuide: Derate current at high ambient. Improve thermal design. Consider extended temperature components.
